params.code:
# THE WAVE, AND THE CLOCK THAT LOOKS AT IT. `f` is how fast the world is wiggling; `fs`
# is how often you bother to look. Everything that follows is a consequence of those two
# numbers and nothing else.
f = 7.0     #@param float min=0.2 max=20 step=0.1
fs = 10     #@param int min=2 max=30
seconds = 1.0   #@param float min=0.5 max=3 step=0.25
# The ghost. Sampling at fs makes every frequency indistinguishable from itself plus any
# multiple of fs: sin(2*pi*f*n/fs) == sin(2*pi*(f - m*fs)*n/fs) for whole m, exactly, for
# every sample. So the wave you will SEE is the one whose frequency is nearest to zero:
m = round(f / fs)
f_alias = f - m * fs            # signed: a negative alias is the same wave, upside down
nyq = fs / 2.0
if abs(f_alias) < 0.05:
    verdict = ("THE WAVE DISAPPEARS: f is a whole multiple of fs, so every sample catches it at "
               "the same phase. The wave is still out there. The machine records a flat line.")
elif abs(f) < nyq - 1e-9:
    verdict = "SAFE: f is below fs/2, and the samples carry the whole wave. The ghost you see IS the original."
elif abs(abs(f) - nyq) < 1e-6:
    verdict = "EXACTLY AT NYQUIST: two samples per cycle. Land them on the zeros and the wave VANISHES."
else:
    verdict = f"ALIASED: you will see {abs(f_alias):.2f} Hz, a wave that was never there."
result = {"f": float(f), "fs": float(fs), "T": float(seconds), "f_alias": f_alias,
          "nyq": nyq, "verdict": verdict, "W": 150.0, "A": 20.0}

# THE TRUTH: the wave as it actually is, drawn at 600 points because a computer cannot
# draw a continuum either — it can only pretend harder than the sampler does. (Every
# point here is a real B-Rep edge, so 'smooth enough' is also a bill: 360, not 3600.)
D = in_0
W, A, T, f = D["W"], D["A"], D["T"], D["f"]
result = [Vector(-W / 2 + (i / 360) * W, 0, A * math.sin(2 * math.pi * f * (i / 360) * T))
          for i in range(361)]

# THE GHOST: the slowest wave that goes through every sample. It agrees with the samples
# EXACTLY — not approximately — so no amount of cleverness downstream can tell the two
# apart. Below Nyquist it lies right on top of the true wave, and that congruence is what
# "sampling loses nothing" means. Above it, it is a different wave entirely, and it is
# the one you will hear, see, and believe.
D = in_0
W, A, T, fa = D["W"], D["A"], D["T"], D["f_alias"]
result = [Vector(-W / 2 + (i / 360) * W, -7,
                 A * math.sin(2 * math.pi * fa * (i / 360) * T))
          for i in range(361)]

# THE STICKS: what the machine actually holds. Not a wave — a list of numbers, one every
# 1/fs of a second. Everything between them is a guess.
D = in_0
n = max(int(D["fs"] * D["T"]) + 1, 2)
result = [max(abs(D["A"] * math.sin(2 * math.pi * D["f"] * (i / D["fs"]))), 0.2)
          for i in range(n)]

D = in_0
W, A, T, fs, f = D["W"], D["A"], D["T"], D["fs"], D["f"]
n = max(int(fs * T) + 1, 2)
out = []
for i in range(n):
    t = i / fs
    v = A * math.sin(2 * math.pi * f * t)
    out.append(Vector(-W / 2 + (t / T) * W, 0, v / 2))
result = out

# THE SAMPLES themselves. This is the whole of the evidence. Both curves go through every
# one of these balls; that is precisely the trouble.
D = in_0
W, A, T, fs, f = D["W"], D["A"], D["T"], D["fs"], D["f"]
n = max(int(fs * T) + 1, 2)
result = [Vector(-W / 2 + ((i / fs) / T) * W, 0, A * math.sin(2 * math.pi * f * (i / fs)))
          for i in range(n)]

D = in_0
result = (f"f = {D['f']:.2f} Hz   |   fs = {D['fs']:.0f} Hz   |   Nyquist (fs/2) = {D['nyq']:.1f} Hz\n"
          f"{D['verdict']}\n"
          f"samples taken: {int(D['fs'] * D['T']) + 1} in {D['T']:.1f} s")

size = 3.6  #@param float min=2 max=10 step=0.2
D = in_0
A, W = D["A"], D["W"]
def _up(s, x, z, fs_):
    return extrude(Pos(x, 12, z) * (Plane.XZ * Text(s, font_size=fs_)), amount=0.6)
head = ("the machine records a flat line" if abs(D["f_alias"]) < 0.05 else
        "the ghost IS the wave — nothing was lost"
        if abs(D["f"]) < D["nyq"] - 1e-9 else
        f"the ghost: {abs(D['f_alias']):.2f} Hz, and it was never there")
out = [_up("SAMPLING, AND THE GHOST", 0, A + 30, size * 1.8),
       _up(f"f = {D['f']:.2f} Hz    sampled {D['fs']:.0f} times a second"
           f"    (Nyquist = {D['nyq']:.1f} Hz)", 0, A + 20, size * 1.15),
       _up(head, 0, A + 11, size * 1.15),
       _up("the wave", -W / 2 - 16, 0, size * 0.9),
       _up("what the machine keeps", 0, -A - 14, size * 0.95)]
result = out

params.text:
THE WAVE THAT WAS NEVER THERE.

A wave, and a clock that looks at it `fs` times a second. The balls are the samples: everything the machine will ever know about this wave. The sticks are how it stores them — a list of numbers, and nothing at all in between.

The second curve is the GHOST: the slowest wave that passes through every single sample. Not approximately through them. Exactly. And that is the whole problem — no cleverness downstream can prefer one curve over the other, because both agree with every scrap of evidence there is.

It opens aliased: 7 Hz sampled 10 times a second, and the ghost is a calm 3 Hz wave that looks entirely innocent. (It is drawn a few mm in front of the true wave, or the two would fight for the same pixels.)

TRY THIS — leave fs at 10 and drag `f` down towards 3.
Below 5 Hz (that is fs/2, Nyquist) the ghost stops being a different wave: it takes exactly the shape of the true one. That congruence IS the sampling theorem — below half the sample rate the dots contain the whole wave and it can be recovered perfectly. Above it, drag `f` to 9 and you will see 1 Hz; the faster the truth, the slower the lie.
At f = 10 (exactly fs) every sample lands on the same phase: the ghost is a FLAT LINE. The wave is still there. The machine records silence.
At exactly 5 Hz, two samples per cycle, the balls can land on the zero crossings and the wave disappears as well. Two samples per cycle is the boundary, not a safe place to be.

This is why a wagon wheel spins backwards in a film (24 samples a second, and a spoke that goes further than half a turn between frames), why a striped shirt shimmers on video, and why every ADC on earth has an analogue low-pass filter welded in front of it: you cannot undo aliasing afterwards. You have to refuse to let the frequency in.

groups.title: 3 - WHAT YOU SEE, AND WHAT IS THERE
